approach: the issue scoped its own substance to ONE question — how the ambiguity failure is reported — and kept it out of the CR that removed the label-uniqueness rule so it could be decided on its merits rather than as a rider. So the report shape was grilled with the requester rather than derived: a structured error under a stable code, candidates on stderr, stdout clean, honoring the format flag; resolution at the CLI layer; an id outranks a name; exit 2 means ambiguous on every pane verb

two of the issue's own premises are false and both were verified against source before drafting. 'Resolution needs no new backend capability': the live-pane listing carries no label on either backend — herdr's pane list returns one the adapter drops, and tmux's listing is space-separated so it cannot carry one at all without moving to tab-separation, which is the trap the region-describing route already documents twenty lines below it. So the CR does grow the listing type on both backends

the load-bearing subtlety is tmux's hostname default: tmux has no unset pane title, so without reusing the existing title-differs-from-host rule every untouched pane would report the SAME label and the hostname would resolve to every pane in the session — ambiguity manufactured out of nothing, in the exact feature meant to resolve it

exit 2 amends the output contract's 0/1 code set. Taken on prior art rather than taste: grep, POSIX test (normative), diff, expr and pgrep all put a couldn't-answer in the status, and systemctl is-active is the counter-case — it prints the same word for a stopped unit and a missing one, leaving only the status to tell them apart. That contract is shared with two sibling projects, so the amendment is scoped to this bin and the cross-adopter question is filed rather than decided here

why.floor: none. The one floor this CR could have tripped is Clearance, and it does not fire: the suite edit is purely additive — 16 added, 0 modified, 0 removed, 90 unchanged, addOnly true — so the frozen file self-clears and no re-open is needed. Verified structurally rather than by line diff, and verified TWICE for cause: the first check was run against a stale remote ref that resolved to a 77-scenario base, and answered addOnly true by luck. Re-run against the true tip after rebasing: same verdict, this time earned. No frozen scenario is narrowed, rewritten or deleted; the frozen bare-send scenario (exits 1, stdout empty) is untouched and disjoint from the new exit-2 path, since it fails on incomplete input rather than on a locator that resolved to no single pane. Compatibility: the code set gains a value, and exit 2 arises only where a name matched 2+ panes — a case that could not previously be expressed, since no verb took a name — so no existing invocation changes its status.
why.blast: medium — two nodes, one of them a REFERENCE contract shared beyond this repo. mux/ gains the addressing use case and 16 scenarios; axi/ has principle #6's exit-code set amended from 0/1 to 0/1/2 and two consequential corrections (#8's parenthetical, which justified bare send's exit 1 with the now-false claim that there is no third code; and the impl-trails-the-contract bullet). The axi amendment is the real radius: that node records cyberplace and universal-plugin as adopters of the SAME contract, so a third code is a divergence until they adopt or decline it. Scoped to this bin, hedged in the node, and filed as a follow-up rather than decided here — matching #8, which diverged first on identical terms. No production code changes; the implementation lands at deliver.
why.novelty: high — the issue's own sketch is wrong twice, and the second one is load-bearing. 'Resolution reads the live pane list, so it needs no new backend capability': the listing type carries NO label on either backend. herdr's pane list returns one the adapter drops on the floor; tmux's listing is space-separated and recovers cwd only by rejoining the tail, so it cannot carry a label at all without moving to tab-separation — which is the exact trap the region-describing route documents twenty lines further down the same file. So the CR does grow the listing on both backends. The subtler one: tmux has no unset pane title and defaults it to the HOSTNAME, so a listing that reported the raw title would label every untouched pane in the session identically, and the hostname would then resolve to all of them — this feature manufacturing ambiguity out of nothing, in the exact path meant to resolve it. The existing title-differs-from-host rule must be reused, not reinvented. Also settled against the issue: it frames the ambiguity report as an open question with no precedent; the prior art is in fact unanimous and splits on whether a total order exists — git, Docker, kubectl and brew all pick silently across tiers, while git short SHAs, tmux targets and brew cross-tap formulae all refuse and enumerate among peers. Our id-then-name ladder and our name-vs-name refusal are the conventional answer, not an invention. Exit 2 likewise: grep, POSIX test (normative), diff, expr and pgrep all reserve a code for couldn't-answer, and systemctl is-active is the counter-case that kills the stdout-word alternative. Uniqueness was considered and refused on the issue's own grounds.
why.confidence: high — FOUR cold spec-judge rounds, each a fresh context re-deriving rather than confirming. R1 caught that the branch was 13 commits behind and that the addOnly verdict had been computed against a stale ref, so the freeze self-clear was unverified at the moment it was claimed; it also caught two scenarios inert against the wrong impl they exist to catch (the verb outline and the single-match scenario each fixed a world of exactly ONE live pane, so a resolver ignoring the name and taking the sole pane passed all nine rows) and one asserting a mechanism with no observable counter-case (a syntax-sniffer passed it). R2, on the rebased tree, passed builder and oracle but caught the spec claiming in the PAST tense that the structured error was 'realized today' and 'built' — on a spec-only round with zero src changes, in the very section whose job is telling a reader what ships; no test could catch it, the artifact being prose. R3 passed all three lenses on the contract but caught the same false-built class RECURRING in this CR's own ledger, because the R2 fix had been applied to the two instances named rather than swept for; it also caught three correction lines written to the durable ledger, which holds only leash/gate/followup, where every other shard in the corpus gets the tier right. R4 confirmed the fixes on a freshly-fetched base, re-ran the structural check and the mechanical engines (check-spec-state, check-suite both OK), swept every touched artifact for the false-built class and found it dead, ran the miss test adversarially over all 16 scenarios and the pairwise sweep across the full 106-scenario file plus layout.feature's 97, and returned all three lenses PASS, ALIGNED true, no blocker. Two judge claims were checked and REFUSED rather than obeyed: R3's demand that correction.cause hold a closed enum (the corpus writes prose in cause and carries the vocabulary in correction-kind, and R4 adjudicated that following the corpus was right and the governance drift is a doctrine-loop matter); and R4's own claim that a missing seq on ledger lines is a corpus-wide gap (9 of 11 shards carry seq on every line — it is the norm, and this shard now conforms). Self-asserted within the auto-spec leash — ratify or kick back.

title: A third exit code diverges from the other adopters of the same output contract
note: The output-contract node records that two sibling projects adopted the same contract, and this CR adds a third exit code — a locator that did not resolve to one subject — to a set that contract fixes at success and failure. The amendment is scoped to one bin, so the contract now has adopters that disagree about what a status code can mean. The question belongs to the contract rather than to any one adopter: whether the code set is shared at all, and if it is, whether a predicate's couldn't-answer earns a code or must stay in prose. Prior art favours the code — grep, POSIX test, diff, expr and pgrep all reserve one — and the counter-case is systemctl is-active, which prints the same word for a stopped unit and a missing one and leaves only the status to tell them apart. Nothing here forces the other adopters either way.

title: Every error path but the ambiguity report is specified to stay free text, so the structured-error clause lands on one path
note: The ambiguity report is contracted as the first structured error on this surface because it is the first caller that actually needs the shape — a stable code, machine-readable candidates, honoring the output format. Every other error is specified to keep routing through the helper that writes free text and exits, ignoring the format flag, so a caller cannot tell one failure from another without parsing prose. Converting the rest is a contract-wide job with its own blast radius across every verb's error path, which is why it was not ridden in on an addressing change. Note that nothing on this surface emits a structured error today at all: the one path above is specified here and built at deliver.

title: The pane listing knows each pane's label but never shows it, so a name is not discoverable from the listing
note: Addressing a pane by name shipped, and the listing now carries the label that makes it possible, but the listing's own table does not show it — so a caller cannot learn from the listing which names are typeable. The column was built and then removed rather than shipped: that row already sits at the output contract's three-to-four field ceiling, and adding a fifth would have broken a bar this same change was amending elsewhere. Which four fields earn the slots, or whether the label belongs behind the full-detail escape hatch the contract already reserves for exactly this, is a question for that contract rather than a rider on an addressing change. Discovery is not wholly lost meanwhile: an applied layout's manifest reports every label it created, and the ambiguity report names each candidate's label when a name collides.

why.floor: none at the impl gate. The CR trips no floor at all: the suite edit was purely additive (16 added, 0 modified, 0 removed) and stayed so through deliver — re-checked on the merged tree after the rebase, still addOnly. No frozen scenario was narrowed, rewritten or deleted, and none was added at this gate. Every defect found was fixed in code or by removing scope, never by editing the contract to fit the implementation. Compatibility: exit 2 arises only where a name matched two or more panes, which no invocation could express before this CR because no verb took a name — so no existing call changes its status, and the seam is untouched, adapters still receiving concrete ids.
why.blast: medium — the seam's listing type, both adapters, and the CLI's whole pane-verb surface, with no change to any adapter contract. The tmux listing had to move space-separated to tab-separated before it could carry a label at all, and the title-differs-from-host rule was extracted and shared rather than written a second time. RE-GATED on the merged tree: the target advanced 7 commits mid-delivery, including a refactor of session.tmux.ts — the same file this CR rewrites — so the branch was re-rebased and this gate run on the merged tree before any push. The rebase reported no conflicts, which was treated as suspicious rather than lucky and checked: the judge confirmed the tmux changes compose with that refactor and nothing of it was clobbered. Scope was CUT at this gate rather than defended: the delivery had added a label column to the pane listing, which is not in the frozen contract and takes that row to five fields against the output contract's three-to-four ceiling — removed and filed.
why.novelty: high — the delivery surfaced a control-flow bug the contract forced out, invisible to reading. Exiting from inside the resolver only appeared to work by relying on process.exit never returning, and the layout-save verb's catch-all would have flattened the ambiguity's exit 2 into exit 1 — a frozen scenario silently violated on one verb while every other verb passed. The ambiguity is now a thrown typed error reported at the verb boundary, and the judge proved the fix load-bearing by mutating each catch-all's rethrow independently and watching exactly that verb's row fail. The second finding was the producer's own: its mutation harness reported a kill where vitest had eaten the filter as a flag, so ZERO tests ran and the nonzero exit read as a pass — a false kill, self-caught by a count check. It is the exact failure mode this CR kept hitting in another costume, and the reason the judge re-ran the mutations by hand instead of reading the table.
why.confidence: high — cold sdd-impl-judge, fresh context, re-deriving every scenario's oracle from the adapter and CLI source rather than from the contract's prose or the producer's report. It re-ran pnpm verify itself (545 tests, uncached), re-ran the structural check against a freshly-fetched base (merge-base identical to origin/main, so no stale-ref repeat of the spec gate's first round), and ran the scenario bridge as an independent cross-check: 16/16 BOUND and PASS, agreeing with its hand read. It refused to trust the producer's 17-row mutation table on the producer's own disclosure of a false kill, and re-ran 8 mutations by hand with non-zero test counts confirmed on every run — id-first precedence, the ambiguity threshold, the exit code, BOTH catch-all rethrow sites, the inverted host rule, the tab-vs-space split, and herdr's label — all killed. It read every bound test's BODY rather than its title, looking for the retitled-test-over-claims shape that bit an earlier CR here, and found none. On scope it verified the four things the delivery was told not to touch: fail() unswept, the seam's types unchanged with adapters never seeing a label, exists's live/gone stdout contract intact, and the nine modified pre-existing tests still asserting toEqual rather than weakened to toContain — the new drives() helper filters only the resolution read every verb now issues, not an assertion. Absorption clean: no literal from any Given is special-cased in production. IMPLEMENTATION_PASS true, no blocker. Its one observation — the unspecified label column — was acted on rather than deferred, and cut. Self-asserted within the auto-spec leash; the spec gate of this CR was ratified in-session by the requester.
